query: recent randomized controlled trials comparing regional versus general anesthesia in geriatric hip fracture patients regarding mortality, morbidity, and healthcare costs

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthetic Considerations in the Geriatric Population -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Intraoperatively, the surgical and anesthetic plans should minimize known risks for elderly patients. General anesthesia in geriatric patients is associated with acute respiratory failure, lengthier hospital stays, and higher mortality in hip fractures. Some studies suggest spinal anesthesia to be a safer primary anesthetic, but more research is needed to evaluate this hypothesis. [76] [Level II]

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthetic Considerations in the Geriatric Population -- Clinical Significance -- Neuraxial Anesthesia
retrieved_docs.content: Several aspects of geriatric care hint toward the overall utility of neuraxial anesthesia to improve outcomes for certain surgeries. One of the most common surgeries performed almost exclusively on elderly patients, repair of fall-related hip fractures, lends itself well to spinal anesthesia. Compared to general anesthesia, Neuraxial techniques are associated with fewer pulmonary complications in patients with lung disease. Decreased requirement of sedating medications may decrease the risk of postoperative delirium. Evidence supports decreased discharge to skilled nursing facilities following total hip replacement for patients undergoing spinal anesthesia rather than general anesthesia. [53] [54] [55]

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthetic Considerations in the Geriatric Population -- Clinical Significance -- Mortality
retrieved_docs.content: Many different attempts and strategies have been undertaken to quantify morbidity and mortality risks in older patients in previous years. Most have discovered age to be an independent risk factor, along with the invasiveness of the surgery and baseline comorbidities. For outpatient surgery, a Medicare database review discovered a mortality rate of 0.23% on the day of surgery and a 0.66% 30-day mortality rate, with higher risk associated with ages over 85. For inpatient noncardiac surgery within the Veteran Affairs system, patients over the age of 80 had an 8% 30-day mortality risk, with a 5% increase in relative risk for each year over 80. Additional mortality risk factors include diagnosis of ischemic or nonischemic heart failure, emergency surgery, invasive surgeries (abdominal surgery, aortic aneurysm repair, and thoracic surgery), and delay in emergent surgical intervention. Even minor procedures such as colonoscopies and cataract surgeries place patients at significant risks, and thorough efforts should be taken to minimize complications. [72] [1] [73] [74]

retrieved_docs.title: Periprosthetic Proximal Femur Fractures -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Total hip arthroplasty (THA) offers a reproducible alternative to individuals who have painful arthritis of the hip. The advancements in medicine have improved patient outcomes in arthroplasty and decreased hospital length of stay. Cooperation with the anesthesia department has revolutionized perioperative and postoperative pain management to limit narcotic use. [1] Interprofessional preoperative management with the primary care team seeks to optimize a patient before surgery or delay patients unfit for elective arthroplasty. Improved patient selection and widespread availability of implants have allowed primary total hip arthroplasty to be performed in patients of a wide age range and often completed as an outpatient surgery. The desire for primary total hip arthroplasty is predicted to rise from 329,000 to 572,000 operations in 2030, a 174% rise from 2005. [2] A focus on individual health and preventative medicine combined with medical advancements, patients are surpassing previously established life expectancies. [3] The increased utilization in total hip arthroplasty has led to a concurrent increase in periprosthetic fractures. [4] Operative fixation of a periprosthetic fracture is taxing surgery that is best suited for surgeons with a significant volume of related procedures and understanding of the complexities of operative planning.

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Opioids in Anesthetic Practice -- Clinical Significance -- Orthopedic
retrieved_docs.content: Spinal anesthesia is beneficial for hip fracture surgery, particularly in the elderly, and a 2005 best practice review recommended regional anesthesia whenever possible for this type of surgery. [47] Benefits cited include decreased mortality, deep vein thrombosis, pulmonary complications, and transfusion requirements. ITM has also been used as part of multimodal anesthesia after total knee arthroplasty, with patients having lower pain scores at 12 hours postoperatively and lower systemic opioid use up to 48 hours postoperatively, compared with controls. [48]

retrieved_docs.title: Accountable Care Organization -- Issues of Concern -- Bundled Payment Care Initiatives (BPCIs)
retrieved_docs.content: BPCIs have become increasingly popular in various selective fields of predictable surgeries (i.e., elective procedures) such as total joint replacements. [5] [6] Furthermore, in future fields and evolving areas across subspecialties like orthopedic surgery, BPCI models are potentially being implemented and/or becoming experimental in diagnostic related groups such as hip fracture patients, elective cervical spine procedures (ACDFs), and isolated surgical extremity trauma. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthetic Considerations in the Geriatric Population -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe physiologic changes in elderly patients and their relevance to perioperative care. Identify risk factors for perioperative morbidity and mortality in elderly patients and explain the importance of a geriatric-focused preoperative assessment. Review anesthetic techniques and intraoperative management of elderly patients. Summarize common perioperative geriatric complications and their management by an interprofessional team. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Six Sigma Method -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Using Six Sigma in hip fracture surgery resulted in improved timely surgical care, better detection of complications, and reduced resource consumption with shorter lengths of stay and lower hospital costs. [16] In a study of patients with end-stage renal disease, the use of the Six Sigma methodology showed a reduction in the incidence of depression and anxiety in patients at discharge. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthetic Considerations in the Geriatric Population -- Issues of Concern -- Endocrine and Metabolism
retrieved_docs.content: Heat production, insulation, and the ability to thermoregulate commonly decline with age. Many common procedures in the elderly, such as exploratory laparotomy or surgical repair of a hip fracture, can result in significant blood and insensible fluid loss and limit the available surface area of forced-air warmers. Incorporating temperature goals into the anesthetic plan and keeping the patient's normothermic decreases serious complications, including dysrhythmias, infection, and delayed wound healing. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthetic Considerations in the Geriatric Population -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Finally, the incidence of Postoperative Cognitive Dysfunction is increasing each year. Early detection and thorough postoperative assessment of cognition changes should be a joint effort by physicians and nurses. Guidelines should be implemented at the institutional level and taught to all perioperative employees to improve neurologic outcomes. [77] [Level I]

retrieved_docs.title: Ultrasound-Guided Fascia Iliaca Compartment Block -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The fascia iliaca compartment block is clinically significant as an effective, relatively low-risk regional anesthesia technique for providing analgesia to the hip, thigh, and knee, particularly in patients with hip fractures, femoral shaft fractures, or undergoing hip arthroplasty. Effective pain management is essential in the emergency setting, particularly for lower extremity injuries such as hip fractures. Traditional pharmacologic approaches, including opioids, NSAIDs, and acetaminophen, present limitations due to adverse effects, contraindications, or insufficient relief. This block offers a targeted, opiate-sparing technique that can provide rapid and sustained analgesia. [9] Administered under ultrasound guidance, this procedure has demonstrated high success rates, minimal complications, and significant reductions in pain scores and morphine use. [10] [11] By targeting the femoral, lateral femoral cutaneous, and obturator nerves—often with minimal risk of motor blockade—it reduces reliance on systemic opioids, thereby minimizing complications such as respiratory depression, delirium, and constipation, especially in elderly or medically fragile patients. This block is especially valuable for older adults with comorbidities, in whom systemic analgesics may carry added risk. Useful in preoperative, intraoperative, and postoperative settings, it improves patient comfort, mobility, and rehabilitation participation while supporting enhanced recovery protocols. The ultrasound-guided approach increases accuracy, decreases complication rates, and allows use in settings ranging from the emergency department to perioperative care, making it a versatile tool in multimodal analgesia strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthetic Considerations in the Geriatric Population -- Issues of Concern -- Preoperative Assessment
retrieved_docs.content: Frailty is a general characterization of a patient's decline in organ function and physiologic reserve, as well as the accumulation of comorbidities. Grip strength, weight loss, gait assessment, cognitive tests, and medical history (including recent falls) are typical variables measured as frailty indicators. In other words, frailty is a preoperative stratification of perioperative vulnerability and correlates to increased mortality, lengthier hospital admission, and discharge to a skilled nursing facility. Several validated screening tools show a correlation between frailty and poor clinical outcomes such as mortality and 30-day postoperative complications. Many frailty assessment tools are chosen and implemented at the hospital level, but an understanding of alternative frailty criteria allows for more accurate risk stratification in particular patient populations. As an example, frailty assessment tools that include grip strength may miscategorize a patient with an isolated upper extremity nerve injury in his or her dominant hand. Along with other information obtained during the preoperative evaluation, frailty assessment can guide anesthetic and surgical plans, plan postoperative admissions and discharges, and anticipate postoperative delirium. When correctly assessed, frailty should also be a sensitive indicator for the necessity of in-depth conversations concerning complication risks, likely outcomes, goals of care, and alternatives to surgery. [41] [42] [43]

retrieved_docs.title: Intertrochanteric Femur Fracture -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The main concern of intertrochanteric fractures is the 20% to 30% mortality risk in the first year following fracture, with males having a higher mortality rate compared to females. If the fracture is not operatively treated within two days of injury, mortality risk increases. Other factors increasing mortality include age greater than 85 years, two or more pre-existing medical conditions, ASA classification (ASA III and IV increases mortality).  Early medical optimization and co-management with medical hospitalists or geriatricians can improve outcomes. The most predictive factor of functional outcomes following operative treatment is a pre-injury function, age, and dementia. With stable fracture patterns, more than half of patients will regain pre-injury walking function and pre-fracture level of activity of daily living function. Unstable fracture patterns tend to have worse outcomes when compared to the stable fracture patterns, with most patients experiencing a decline in mobility. Immediate postoperative management is imperative as older adults with these fractures have a five- to eight-time increase in risk for all causes of mortality during the 90-day postoperative period. [10] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Vertebral Augmentation -- Nursing, Allied Health, and Interprofessional Team Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: VA has demonstrated an excellent safety profile, significant survival benefits, and notable improvements in patient function. Meta-analyses show that patients undergoing VA are 22% less likely to die within 10 years compared to those receiving nonoperative management. [31] Importantly, this survival advantage does not come at the cost of increased adjacent or remote vertebral fractures. [5] These findings reinforce the value of VA in managing VCFs, irrespective of the time elapsed since the fracture. However, vigilant follow-up remains essential, especially in "red flag" symptoms such as persistent or refractory pain, signs of infection, dyspnea, chest pain, or any neurologic changes that may suggest procedural complications or evolving pathology.

Internal‑fixation procedures (identified as “تثبيت داخلي للكسر”) are associated with multiple treatment categories: “fracture or dislocation of hip and femur,” “fracture or dislocation of lower extremity (other than hip or femur),” and “fracture or dislocation of radius and ulna.” These associations emphasize that bone quality influences surgical decision‑making for both hip and peripheral limb injuries. Within the osteoporosis SMQ, additional members such as “جراحة مفصل الورك” (hip joint surgery), “إعاضة رأس الفخذ” (hip head prosthesis), and “كسور متعددة” (multiple fractures) illustrate the spectrum of interventions and clinical manifestations that arise in patients with compromised bone health. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1599
retrieved_docs.content: Internal fixation of fracture (تثبيت داخلي للكسر) and hip head replacement (إعاضة رأس الفخذ) are both classified as members of the osteoporosis/low bone mass category (هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام (SMQ)). This indicates that these surgical interventions are specifically associated with the management of patients who have osteoporosis or reduced bone density, reflecting their role in treating fractures and hip pathology that result from weakened bone structure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 823
retrieved_docs.content: Osteoporosis/low bone mass (هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام (SMQ)) is a condition marked by diminished bone density that predisposes patients to skeletal fragility. Internal fixation of fractures (تثبيت داخلي للكسر), hip head replacement (إعاضة رأس الفخذ), and the occurrence of multiple fractures (كسور متعددة) are all recognized as clinical entities that belong to the osteoporosis/low bone mass category. These procedures and fracture patterns commonly arise because weakened bone is less able to withstand normal stresses, necessitating surgical stabilization or joint replacement. Effective management of osteoporosis/low bone mass therefore aims to strengthen bone, reduce the incidence of such fractures, and limit the need for invasive orthopedic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2646
retrieved_docs.content: Hip head prosthesis (إعاضة رأس الفخذ) and multiple fractures (كسور متعددة) are both recognized as clinical manifestations within the standardized MedDRA query for osteoporosis/low bone mass (هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام (SMQ)). This relationship highlights that patients with osteoporosis or reduced bone density are prone to severe skeletal complications, including the need for hip head replacement procedures and the occurrence of multiple fractures. Recognizing these associations is important for clinicians to assess fracture risk, consider surgical interventions such as hip prostheses, and implement comprehensive management strategies aimed at preserving bone health and preventing further skeletal injury.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1551
retrieved_docs.content: Other hip procedures are categorized as a sub‑type of Procedures and interventions, Not Elsewhere Classified, which in turn is placed under the broader category of إجراء تدخلي (interventional procedures). In addition, Other hip procedures are also classified as a subset of hip replacement; total and partial, linking them directly to the spectrum of total and partial hip replacement surgeries. This hierarchical organization situates Other hip procedures within both a general framework of miscellaneous interventional procedures and a specific orthopedic context of hip replacement interventions, facilitating clearer clinical classification and patient‑care planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 1131
retrieved_docs.content: Closed fracture of the intracapsular section of the femur is mapped to a fracture of the neck of the femur (hip), indicating that these two descriptions refer to the same injury pattern. The fracture of the neck of the femur (hip) is classified within its own category and includes osteoporosis/low bone mass (هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام (SMQ)) as a member, highlighting the strong association between hip neck fractures and underlying bone fragility. Internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction is positioned as a treatment option for multiple anatomical regions: it is a child of the treatment categories for fracture or dislocation of the hip and femur, for fracture or dislocation of the lower extremity (excluding the hip and femur), and for fracture or dislocation of the radius and ulna. This procedure is also linked, via an inverse‑isa relationship, to the Arabic concept تثبيت داخلي للكسر (internal fixation of fracture), and is considered a type of internal fixation of the clavicle without fracture reduction. Both تثبيت داخلي للكسر (internal fixation of fracture) and إعاضة رأس الفخذ (hip head replacement) are listed as members of the osteoporosis/low bone mass (هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام (SMQ)) group, underscoring that surgical interventions such as internal fixation and hip head arthroplasty are frequently employed in patients whose fractures are precipitated by compromised bone quality. Overall, the knowledge base connects intracapsular femoral fractures, hip neck fractures, and related surgical treatments to the underlying condition of osteoporosis/low bone mass, emphasizing the importance of recognizing bone fragility when planning fixation or replacement strategies for fractures of the hip, femur, lower extremity, and forearm.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 230
retrieved_docs.content: hip biomechanics. Additionally, “Internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction” is identified as a versatile internal fixation technique applicable to fractures or dislocations of the hip and femur, the radius and ulna, and other lower‑extremity sites, underscoring its role across multiple skeletal regions. Collectively, these entities illustrate an integrated framework for fracture fixation, deformity correction, growth modulation, prosthetic revision, and preventive care in thigh and leg surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1267
retrieved_docs.content: Closed fracture of the intracapsular section of the femur is mapped to the clinical entity “fracture of neck of femur (hip).” The condition “fracture of neck of femur (hip)” serves as a classification for itself and is linked to the SMQ concept “هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام (SMQ),” which denotes osteoporosis or low bone mass. Osteoporosis/low bone mass is therefore recognized as a member within the broader category of hip neck fractures. Several orthopedic interventions and related injury patterns are identified as members of the osteoporosis/low bone mass group: internal fixation of the fracture (“تثبيت داخلي للكسر”), hip joint surgery (“جراحة مفصل الورك”), hip head replacement (“إعاضة رأس الفخذ”), and the occurrence of multiple fractures (“كسور متعددة”). This network of relationships highlights that hip neck fractures are closely associated with underlying bone fragility, and that common surgical treatments for these fractures, as well as the presence of multiple fractures, are considered manifestations or management strategies within the context of osteoporosis or reduced bone density. Understanding these interconnections aids clinicians in recognizing the role of bone quality in hip fracture pathology and in selecting appropriate surgical or therapeutic approaches for patients with compromised skeletal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 1032
retrieved_docs.content: Closed fracture of intracapsular section of femur is mapped to fracture of neck of femur (hip), indicating that these two descriptions refer to the same clinical injury of the proximal femur. Fracture of neck of femur (hip) is listed as having a member relationship with هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام (SMQ), the standardized query that groups adverse events related to osteoporosis or low bone density. In the same SMQ, several procedural and injury concepts are also classified as members: تثبيت داخلي للكسر (internal fixation of a fracture), إعاضة رأس الفخذ (femoral head prosthesis or hip replacement), and كسور متعددة (multiple fractures). Together, these relationships show that hip neck fractures, their surgical management (internal fixation or prosthetic replacement), and the occurrence of multiple fractures are all recognized as clinical manifestations or treatments associated with osteoporosis/low bone density, underscoring the importance of bone health assessment and appropriate orthopedic intervention in patients with compromised skeletal integrity.

retrieved_docs.content: technique across skeletal regions and is grouped with hip‑head replacement and multiple fractures within the osteoporosis/low bone mass (هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام) clinical category, reflecting the heightened need for surgical stabilization in patients with reduced bone density. Collectively, this network of femoral‑focused and lower‑extremity surgical options provides clinicians with comprehensive strategies for fracture stabilization, malunion correction, growth disturbance management, limb‑length modification, prosthetic revision, joint reconstruction, and soft‑tissue repair, thereby supporting optimal functional outcomes across a spectrum of orthopedic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1290
retrieved_docs.content: Other hip procedures are categorized as a subset of both the broad group “Procedures and interventions, Not Elsewhere Classified” and the more specific category “hip replacement; total and partial,” indicating that these procedures are considered part of general uncategorized medical interventions while also being related to total and partial hip replacement surgeries.
